planting.note: Direct sow thickly in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; minutina germinates rapidly in cool conditions and is one of the most cold-tolerant salad greens available. Make successive sowings every 3 weeks from late winter through early spring, then again from late summer through fall.

directions: Direct sow in cool weather in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; minutina (erba stella, or 'star grass') is one of the most unusual and sought-after Italian salad greens, producing rosettes of narrow, deeply toothed, antler-like leaves that have a pleasantly mild, slightly nutty to salty flavor and a satisfying crunch — a standout texture in any mixed salad. Unlike most salad greens, minutina is very tolerant of light frost and can be harvested almost year-round in mild climates; in the Southeast it is a fall-through-spring crop that withstands temperatures down to the mid-20s°F. The leaves are cut at the base and the plant regrows from the crown for multiple harvests. It tolerates partial shade better than most salad crops, making it productive in spots that receive only 3–4 hours of direct sun. Slow to bolt even in cool, bright conditions. Outstanding in the cut-and-come-again salad garden.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, deeply cultivated soil in full sun to partial shade; Turkish rocket has a taproot and dislikes transplanting — sow in its permanent location. Once established it is extremely long-lived and drought tolerant. A perennial vegetable that will produce for decades in the right site.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline, deeply cultivated soil in full sun to partial shade; Turkish rocket is one of the most productive and versatile perennial vegetables for the four-season food garden — a member of the mustard family (Brassicaceae) with an edible flavor profile that shifts through the season. The young spring leaves are used raw in salads with a mild, arugula-like flavor; as they age and before flowering they develop a stronger, mustardy bite better suited to cooking as a potherb. The young flower buds and flowers are eaten like broccoli raab. After flowers open, the distinctive warty, corky, two-seeded pods are produced. The plant is extremely long-lived, drought tolerant, and cold hardy once established, and produces earlier in spring than almost any other perennial vegetable. Self-seeds freely — deadhead after flowering if spread is unwanted. An outstanding low-maintenance perennial vegetable for the permaculture food garden.
sources: Martin Crawford, Creating a Forest Garden (Permanent Publications)
Edible Forest Gardens, Dave Jacke and Eric Toensmeier

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (side shoots from the crown) in spring in well-drained, deeply cultivated, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; arracacha requires 9–12 months from planting to harvest and performs best where temperatures range 57–75°F — it struggles in heat above 85°F and is best suited to mild coastal or highland climates in zones 8–10.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in spring in deeply cultivated, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; arracacha is one of the most important root crops of the South American Andes and is gaining attention among adventurous food gardeners in mild climates for its exceptional flavor — the white to cream-colored roots have a starchy texture and a flavor that combines the best qualities of celery, carrot, and parsnip with a slightly sweet, buttery richness. The roots are boiled, roasted, fried, or used in soups; they do not store well after harvest and are best consumed within a few days. The plant resembles a flat-leafed parsley or celery relative with compound leaves and naturally produces multiple offsets from the crown that can be replanted as cuttings to propagate. It thrives in the cool, humid conditions of zones 8–10 coastal areas and mild highlands where temperatures remain moderate — it is not well suited to the extreme summer heat of the Gulf Coast interior but performs in coastal areas and in partial shade.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division
Lost Crops of the Incas (National Academies Press)

planting.note: Direct sow seed 1/4 inch deep in early spring or start transplants indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; garlic chives can also be grown from divisions of established clumps, which is faster and more reliable than seed. Self-seeds prolifically — deadhead spent flowers diligently to prevent volunteer seedlings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; garlic chives (nira in Japanese cuisine) are a perennial Asian allium with flat, strap-like dark green leaves — distinct from common chives (A. schoenoprasum), which have hollow, cylindrical leaves — and a pleasant, mild garlic-onion flavor that intensifies when cooked. Used prolifically in Chinese and Japanese cooking in dumplings (jiaozi), stir-fries, pancakes, soups, and as a potherb, they can also be blanched to produce yellow 'yellow chives' (nira-ki) with a more delicate flavor. In late summer the plant produces showy clusters of white star-shaped flowers on erect stalks — the flowers are also edible and attractive enough for the ornamental garden. Garlic chives are more heat tolerant than common chives and perform well through zone 9 summers. Cut the entire plant to 2–3 inches several times per season to maintain tender regrowth; divide congested clumps every 3–4 years.

planting.note: Direct sow thickly in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna germinates rapidly and is ready for baby-leaf cutting in as few as 21 days. Make successive sowings every 2–3 weeks through cool weather for continuous harvest.

directions: Direct sow in cool weather in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna is one of the most popular Japanese salad greens and one of the fastest, easiest cool-season crops available, producing deeply cut, feathery, pale green leaves with a mild, slightly peppery, refreshing flavor that is pleasantly tender when harvested young. It is one of the most cold tolerant of all the Asian brassicas and continues growing through light freezes, making it an outstanding fall and winter crop in zones 7–9. Begin harvesting as baby leaf at 21 days by cutting the outer leaves; the plant regrows for multiple harvests before bolting in warm weather. Bolt resistance is better than arugula, and the plant handles light shade well, making it productive in partly shaded plots. Red-leaved mizuna cultivars ('Red Kingdom', 'Mikado Red') add color to salad mixes. An outstanding addition to spring and fall salad mixes, stir-fries, and as a garnish in Japanese cuisine.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pot cuttings in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; water mint thrives in the boggy margins of ponds, streams, and rain gardens in partial shade. It can grow in standing water up to 4 inches deep. Contain in buried pots to control the spreading stolons where spread is unwanted.

directions: Plant in moist to wet soil or at the margin of a pond or stream in partial shade; water mint is the wild European native mint from which the widely planted peppermint (Mentha x piperita) was bred as a hybrid with spearmint, and it retains a strong, cool menthol fragrance in its leaves and stems. It is one of the most shade- and moisture-tolerant of all mints, making it uniquely valuable for the shaded bog garden, rain garden, or pond margin where other herbs struggle. The strongly scented leaves are used exactly like other culinary mints — in teas, as a culinary herb, in drinks, and for fragrance. Dense clusters of lilac-mauve flowers in summer and fall attract an outstanding variety of pollinators, particularly hoverflies, bumblebees, and small native bees. Like all mints, it spreads by underground stolons and should be contained with buried barriers or grown in sunken pots where spread is unwanted.
